tasks.prompt:
Warm-up: Map the two-layer EKS access model. For each of the four scenarios below, state whether the problem is an IAM (Layer 1) problem, an RBAC (Layer 2) problem, or both — and explain your reasoning in one sentence each.

Scenario A: Running 'aws eks update-kubeconfig' succeeds, but every 'kubectl' command returns 'Error from server (Forbidden): pods is forbidden'.
Scenario B: Running 'aws eks update-kubeconfig' fails with 'ResourceNotFoundException: No cluster found'.
Scenario C: 'kubectl get pods' works, but 'kubectl delete deployment my-app' returns 'deployments.apps is forbidden'.
Scenario D: Jenkins can run 'kubectl get pods' in the 'default' namespace but not in the 'production' namespace.
tasks.starter:
# Fill in Layer (IAM / RBAC / Both) and one-sentence reason for each scenario.

# Scenario A:
# Layer: ________
# Reason: ________

# Scenario B:
# Layer: ________
# Reason: ________

# Scenario C:
# Layer: ________
# Reason: ________

# Scenario D:
# Layer: ________
# Reason: ________
tasks.solution:
# Scenario A:
# Layer: RBAC (Layer 2)
# Reason: update-kubeconfig succeeded (IAM authentication passed), but the IAM identity
#         has not been granted any Kubernetes RBAC permissions — it can reach the cluster
#         but cannot perform any actions inside it.

# Scenario B:
# Layer: IAM (Layer 1)
# Reason: The AWS CLI could not find the cluster, meaning the IAM identity lacks
#         eks:DescribeCluster permission or the cluster name/region is wrong —
#         the request never reached Kubernetes at all.

# Scenario C:
# Layer: RBAC (Layer 2)
# Reason: Read access ('get pods') is permitted but destructive write access
#         ('delete deployment') is not — the RBAC Role grants 'get/list/watch'
#         but not 'delete', so this is a fine-grained RBAC permission gap.

# Scenario D:
# Layer: RBAC (Layer 2)
# Reason: RBAC is namespace-scoped via Role/RoleBinding; the Jenkins identity has a
#         RoleBinding granting 'get pods' in 'default' but no RoleBinding in
#         'production', so cross-namespace access is denied.
tasks.explanation: The two-layer model means 'Unauthorized' errors need to be attributed to the correct layer before you can fix them. IAM errors (Layer 1) prevent you from reaching the cluster at all — update-kubeconfig fails, or kubectl cannot contact the API server. RBAC errors (Layer 2) occur after successful authentication: your identity is recognized, but the permitted verbs or resources are limited. Scenario D illustrates a common production pattern: a RoleBinding is namespace-scoped, so the same RBAC Role must be bound in every namespace the identity needs access to (or use a ClusterRoleBinding for cluster-wide access).
hints: Use 'kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource> --namespace <ns>' to test a specific permission without guessing.
The error message 'Error from server (Forbidden)' always means Layer 2 (RBAC). If the API server is unreachable, you get a connection error instead.
'aws eks describe-cluster --name my-cluster --region us-east-1' is a quick IAM layer test — if this CLI call succeeds, your IAM identity has enough permission to interact with the EKS control plane.

tasks.prompt: Core practice A — Connect to your EKS cluster using kubectl. Run the command to update your kubeconfig for the cluster 'my-cluster' in 'us-east-1', then run two verification commands to confirm access. Replace placeholders with your actual cluster name and region.
tasks.starter:
# Step 1: Update kubeconfig
aws eks update-kubeconfig \
  --region ________ \
  --name ________

# Step 2: Verify you can see cluster nodes
kubectl ________

# Step 3: Verify you can list Pods in all namespaces
kubectl ________
tasks.solution:
# Step 1: Update kubeconfig
aws eks update-kubeconfig \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --name my-cluster

# Step 2: Verify you can see cluster nodes
kubectl get nodes

# Step 3: Verify you can list Pods in all namespaces
kubectl get pods -A
tasks.explanation: 'aws eks update-kubeconfig' writes the cluster's API endpoint, the CA certificate for TLS verification, and an authentication command into ~/.kube/config. After running it, kubectl knows where to send requests and how to authenticate using your current AWS identity (IAM user or role). 'kubectl get nodes' confirms you can talk to the cluster and have at least read access to node resources. 'kubectl get pods -A' (the '-A' flag means --all-namespaces) is a broader check — if this returns results you have cluster-wide pod read permission, which is a common indicator that your identity has been correctly mapped and granted permissions.
hints: If 'kubectl get nodes' returns 'Unauthorized', your IAM identity reached the cluster (IAM layer passed) but has no RBAC permissions yet. You need an EKS access entry or aws-auth ConfigMap entry mapping your IAM identity to a Kubernetes group.
To see which cluster kubectl is currently pointing at: kubectl config current-context
If you manage multiple clusters, 'aws eks update-kubeconfig' adds a new context. Switch between them with: kubectl config use-context <context-name>

tasks.prompt: Core practice B — Set up the IAM-to-RBAC mapping for a Jenkins IAM role. Given that the Jenkins EC2 instance has the IAM role 'arn:aws:iam::111122223333:role/jenkins-deploy-role', write: (1) the AWS CLI command to create an EKS access entry mapping that role to the Kubernetes group 'deploy-group', and (2) a Kubernetes ClusterRoleBinding YAML that grants the 'deploy-group' permission to get, list, update, and patch Deployments across all namespaces (bind to a ClusterRole named 'deployment-updater').
tasks.starter:
# Part 1: Create EKS access entry
# Map the Jenkins IAM role to the Kubernetes group 'deploy-group'
aws eks create-access-entry \
  --cluster-name ________ \
  --principal-arn ________ \
  --kubernetes-groups ________

# Part 2: ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding YAML
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: deployment-updater
rules:
  - apiGroups: [________]
    resources: [________]
    verbs: [________]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: deployment-updater-binding
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ________
  name: ________
subjects:
  - kind: ________
    name: ________
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
tasks.solution:
# Part 1: Create EKS access entry
aws eks create-access-entry \
  --cluster-name my-cluster \
  --principal-arn arn:aws:iam::111122223333:role/jenkins-deploy-role \
  --kubernetes-groups deploy-group

# Part 2: ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding YAML
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: deployment-updater
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "update", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: deployment-updater-binding
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: deployment-updater
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: deploy-group
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
tasks.explanation: The EKS access entry ('aws eks create-access-entry') is the modern replacement for the aws-auth ConfigMap. It tells EKS: when a request arrives signed by the IAM role 'jenkins-deploy-role', treat it as a member of the Kubernetes group 'deploy-group'. The ClusterRole defines what that group can do: 'apps' is the API group for Deployments, and the permitted verbs are the minimum needed for 'kubectl apply' and 'kubectl set image'. The ClusterRoleBinding attaches the role to the group. Together, the chain is: IAM role → EKS access entry → Kubernetes group 'deploy-group' → ClusterRoleBinding → ClusterRole → allowed verbs on Deployments. For a namespace-scoped restriction, use Role + RoleBinding instead of ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding.
hints: Deployments belong to the 'apps' API group. Core resources (pods, services, configmaps) use an empty string apiGroup: [""].
After applying the RBAC YAML, test Jenkins' permissions by impersonating the group: kubectl auth can-i update deployments --as-group=deploy-group --as=jenkins
The EKS access entry '--kubernetes-groups' flag must match exactly the 'name' field in the ClusterRoleBinding's subjects — case-sensitive.

tasks.prompt:
Applied challenge — Debug a permission error. After setting up the access entry and RBAC binding from the previous task, Jenkins' pipeline fails with:

  Error from server (Forbidden): deployments.apps "my-app" is forbidden:
  User "system:anonymous" cannot update resource "deployments"
  in API group "apps" in the namespace "production"

Analyze the error message, identify the root cause, and write the corrected 'aws eks update-kubeconfig' invocation and the pipeline snippet that Jenkins should run in the deploy stage to authenticate correctly before running kubectl.
tasks.starter:
# Analyze the error:
# Q1: What does 'User: system:anonymous' tell you about what went wrong?
# Answer: ________

# Q2: What is the likely missing step in the Jenkins pipeline?
# Answer: ________

# Fix: Write the corrected deploy stage snippet that authenticates
# before running kubectl, using the Jenkins EC2 instance IAM role automatically.

stage('Deploy to EKS') {
    steps {
        sh """
          # authentication step
          ________
          # deploy
          kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
        """
    }
}
tasks.solution:
# Q1: 'User: system:anonymous' means Kubernetes received an unauthenticated request
#     — the kubectl command ran without a valid kubeconfig or token, so the API
#     server fell back to treating it as an anonymous user. The Jenkins agent
#     either has no kubeconfig at all, or the kubeconfig does not provide
#     a valid AWS authentication token.

# Q2: The Jenkins pipeline is missing the 'aws eks update-kubeconfig' call before
#     running kubectl. Without it, kubectl has no cluster endpoint or credentials.

# Fixed deploy stage:
stage('Deploy to EKS') {
    steps {
        sh """
          aws eks update-kubeconfig --region us-east-1 --name my-cluster
          kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
        """
    }
}
tasks.explanation: 'system:anonymous' is the Kubernetes identity for unauthenticated requests — it appears when kubectl has no kubeconfig, an invalid one, or an expired token. The fix is to run 'aws eks update-kubeconfig' at the start of the deploy stage to generate (or refresh) the kubeconfig for the Jenkins build workspace. Because the Jenkins EC2 instance has the IAM role 'jenkins-deploy-role' attached, the AWS CLI automatically retrieves credentials from the instance metadata service — no static keys needed. After update-kubeconfig, kubectl's requests are signed with a valid STS token, EKS maps it to 'deploy-group' via the access entry, and the ClusterRoleBinding grants the update permission. 'aws eks update-kubeconfig' is safe to re-run on every build — it is idempotent.
hints: The error says 'namespace production' — if the ClusterRoleBinding from the previous task only covers the 'default' namespace (using a RoleBinding, not a ClusterRoleBinding), Jenkins would also be denied in 'production'. Check whether you used ClusterRoleBinding vs. RoleBinding.
To confirm which AWS identity Jenkins is using inside the build, add: aws sts get-caller-identity — this prints the ARN of the assumed role.
If you see 'system:anonymous' even after update-kubeconfig, check that the kubeconfig file was written to the correct path. On the Jenkins agent, $HOME/.kube/config may differ from where you expect.
